items.mistake: Reading sys.argv manually and slicing it.
items.fix: Declare arguments with argparse and call parse_args().
items.why: Manual parsing skips validation, defaults, help and error messages.
items.example:
```python
# BAD: brittle, no --help, no validation
path = sys.argv[1]; limit = int(sys.argv[2])

# GOOD
args = parser.parse_args()
path, limit = args.path, args.limit
```

items.mistake: Expecting --batch-size to be accessible as args['batch-size'].
items.fix: Use the attribute args.batch_size (dashes become underscores).
items.why: parse_args returns a Namespace; option names are normalized to valid identifiers.
items.example:
```python
parser.add_argument('--batch-size', type=int, default=32)
args = parser.parse_args()
print(args.batch_size)   # NOT args['batch-size']
```

items.mistake: Using type=bool for an on/off flag.
items.why: type=bool('false') is True for any non-empty string, so it never turns off.
items.example:
```python
# BAD: --verbose false → args.verbose is still True!
parser.add_argument('--verbose', type=bool)
# GOOD: presence-only flag
parser.add_argument('--verbose', action='store_true')
```

items.mistake: Forgetting type= so numbers arrive as strings.
items.fix: Set type=int / type=float on numeric options.
items.why: Without type=, every value is a str and arithmetic or comparisons misbehave.
items.example:
```python
parser.add_argument('--k', default=5)         # BAD: args.k is '5'
parser.add_argument('--k', type=int, default=5)  # GOOD: args.k is 5
```
